senses.definition: used when one wants to explain how a series of events made something happen without giving away many details

examples.x: I just wanted to clean out the garage, but one thing led to another, and I ended up reorganizing the entire house.
I started by asking for some gardening tips, and one thing led to another, resulting in a full-scale landscaping project.
I offered to help with the event decorations, and one thing led to another, and I found myself planning the entire party.
As we brainstorm ideas for the project, one thing leads to another, and we're considering a complete rebranding.

senses.definition: (of an action or decision) to allow for many possibilities, particularly those that were previously unavailable

examples.x: The company's new policy to allow employees to work from home opened the floodgates for a more flexible and diverse workforce.
Lowering the interest rates on loans may open the floodgates for more people to invest in real estate.
The release of the affordable smartphone opened the floodgates to a broader market of consumers who could now afford the technology.
The government's decision to reduce trade barriers with neighboring countries is expected to open the floodgates to increased international commerce.
The announcement of a scholarship program for underprivileged students will open the floodgates of educational opportunities for many young individuals.

senses.definition: to create a situation in which it is easier for one to do something or for something to happen

examples.x: Her years of hard work and dedication paved the way for her promotion to the role of department manager.
The invention of the internet paved the way for a revolution in communication and information sharing.
The diplomatic negotiations between the two countries paved the way for a historic peace agreement.
Investing in a good education can pave the way for a successful career and financial stability.
His groundbreaking research on renewable energy sources has paved the way for a more sustainable future.

senses.definition: to be the direct cause of something, particularly something unpleasant

examples.x: The detective believed that a personal grudge was at the bottom of the crime.
The lack of funding for the project was at the bottom of its failure to launch successfully.
After careful investigation, it was clear that miscommunication was at the bottom of the team's conflicts.
In the world of politics, hidden agendas can often be at the bottom of major policy decisions.
A misunderstanding between the two friends was at the bottom of their recent falling out.

word_list.pronunciation: biňź dňĆa än t ä ňĆ…õsbňąiňź

senses.definition: to be heavily dependent on someone or something

examples.x: He claimed his problems were down to the media.
The success of the project was down to her hard work and dedication.
The team's victory was down to their excellent teamwork and strategy.
His recovery is down to the support of his family and the medical team.
The decision to expand the business was down to the company's growth and opportunities in the market.

senses.definition: with no particular or apparent reason

examples.x: He enjoys playing the piano for the sake of it, finding relaxation in the music.
We decided to go for a walk in the park for the sake of it, enjoying the pleasant weather.
She often bakes cookies for the sake of it, not necessarily for a special occasion.
Sometimes, he'll paint a picture for the sake of it, just to express his creativity.
They had a spontaneous picnic in the backyard for the sake of it, making the most of their time together.

senses.definition: to figure out why things are the way they are and be able to identify the reason

examples.x: She couldn't quite explain what was wrong with the painting, but she could put her finger on the issue after a closer look.
The detective had a hunch about the case, but he couldn't put his finger on the key evidence until he reexamined the crime scene.
When it comes to improving customer satisfaction, it's essential to put your finger on the specific aspects that need attention.
I know something has changed in our team dynamics, but I can't quite put my finger on the exact moment it happened.

senses.definition: used to refer to where the all the trouble comes from or where things get difficult

examples.x: When she talked about her struggles with time management, she pointed out where the shoe pinches ‚Äď meeting deadlines for her assignments.
In the project discussion, team members were encouraged to share their concerns and explain where the shoe pinches in the current process.
The employees voiced their frustrations about workplace inefficiencies and pointed out where the shoe pinches in their daily tasks.
John could finally discuss his financial challenges and explain where the shoe pinches ‚Äď his mounting credit card debt.
During the therapy session, he described his emotional struggles and revealed where the shoe pinches in his personal life.

senses.definition: the strong belief of someone being responsible for or guilty of something

examples.x: According to recent polls, the finger of blame is pointing squarely at the president for the recent economic downturn.
The authorities were quick to place the finger of blame on the rival gang, but they needed more information to confirm their involvement.

senses.definition: a situation in which one finds it impossible to determine which one of two things is responsible for the occurrence of another or existed before the other one did

examples.x: When discussing climate change and its impact on wildlife, it's often a chicken and egg situation to establish which came first.

senses.definition: to blame someone or hold them accountable for something bad that has happened

examples.x: The media often points the finger at celebrities for societal issues, even when they may not be directly responsible.

word_list.pronunciation: sňą…õt √į…ô stňąe…™d í f…Ēňź…Ļ ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É

senses.definition: to do something that makes it possible or easier for something else to occur

examples.x: The successful negotiations set the stage for a new era of international cooperation.
The company's innovative marketing strategies have set the stage for significant sales growth.
Her dedication and hard work in school have set the stage for a promising career in medicine.
The government's policies on renewable energy aim to set the stage for a more sustainable future.
The groundbreaking research in this field is setting the stage for future scientific discoveries.

word_list.pronunciation: sňąo ä plňą√¶nt √į…ô sňąiňźdz  Ćv ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É

senses.definition: to cause the development of a particular idea or feeling

examples.x: His inspirational book sowed the seeds of hope in the hearts of readers, encouraging them to pursue their dreams.
The teacher's kind words planted the seeds of self-confidence in her students, helping them overcome their insecurities.
The art exhibit aimed to sow the seeds of environmental awareness and activism in the minds of its visitors.
Through their charitable work, the organization is planting the seeds of compassion and empathy in the community.
The passionate debate among the scientists sowed the seeds of a revolutionary breakthrough in the field of technology.
